summary: The employee is tasked with designing a relational model for a calendar application. They begin by introducing themselves, then clarify the required features for the application, such as adding events, reminders, time zones, and inviting other users. The employee then starts outlining the core entities for the database schema, including users, calendars, events, and notifications, discussing their attributes and initial data types. The employee details a database design for a calendar system, explaining relationships between users, calendars, events, and notifications. They discuss the use of join tables for many-to-many relationships and for storing additional metadata like permissions and roles. The interviewer asks clarifying questions about design choices, such as using join tables over foreign keys, and probes how the design would adapt to support team calendars. The employee discusses database design for team calendars, focusing on permissions, roles, and scaling considerations. The interviewer then debriefs the employee, asking for self-reflection and providing feedback on their design approach, including suggestions for improvement like indexing.
quality_flags: Transcript cut off at end of chunk.
Transcript cut off at the end of the chunk.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Designed a relational model for a calendar application. Hi everyone, we're so excited today to be doing another Exponent mock interview today with Connor. Connor, will you go ahead and introduce yourself?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, hi everyone. My name is Connor Lindsay. I'm a software engineer at Grafana Labs, currently working on our open-source observability software.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, so let's get started. Our question today is: design a relational model for a calendar application. To get started, I just want to ask a couple of clarifying questions. I'm talking about a calendar application, I'd love to clarify what features specifically that we're looking to include. I'm assuming things like events, maybe notifications or reminders. Could you provide some more information there?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Sure. The user should be able to add events, reminders, the time zone, and be able to eventually invite other users to the events.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Awesome. So, looking through these requirements, I think the first thing that I like to think through is the different entities or types of data that we're going to be working with. So, of course, starting off, there is going to be a calendar. It's going to have some information associated with that. Of course, there will be users. Depending on the scope of the application, I might consider breaking that out into different models as well, maybe like users, accounts, profiles, etc., to split up their personal information, their login credentials. Maybe this is a paid service and there's separate billing information. I think for now, we'll just have users. There will be events, of course, and then I think we'll want some sort of notification or reminder model as well. My guess is that it would be good to split out from that like a notification channel, and this would represent an email or SMS or other channel that we're going to be sending notifications through. So, I think that covers most of the entities that we're going to be working with. The next thing that I want to kind of think through from there, as we build out the model more, is what are the different data types on there, and especially what are the relationships between these? I think that's where most of the complexity and the interesting aspects of this model will come into play. So, we'll start by writing these out. I'll start here with a user. I'll have an ID. This will be the primary key, probably serial, probably a UUID, just kind of automatically generated by whatever database we end up working with. In addition to that, we'll have some other information associated with the user, something like the email or name, which would be a string, the email as well, and then anything else that we want to include with the user. I think that's good for now. I'll build out another table for the calendar. Similarly, it'll also have an ID, hierarchy, everything pretty consistent across that. With the calendar, thinking about what types of metadata there would be associated with it, we'll have probably a name and maybe a description tied to it. And likely there would be some settings. For the moment, based on the requirements, I think we would just have time zone. And this might be, depending on the database, there might be a time zone or a date time type that we'd be able to use here. So, I'll leave that there for now. Maybe that's something that you'd build out a different settings table for or have some other data structure to represent those, but I think that looks good for calendar. I'll build out the events table. Same thing for the key. Then for an event, probably having a name, some description associated with it. And then thinking through the time, there will probably be a start and end date, date time. So, that'll be the day that it's on, the time that it starts, and then the end will be the same thing. But actually, one thing to probably consider is that this may be an all-day event, and that might be handled a little bit differently. One thing I'm kind of thinking through is what are going to be the access patterns with this database, with this model? And I think trying to think ahead of how I'd be working with this on the application itself, I would rather have some more information here in the database, whether it's all day or not, versus trying to calculate, 'Okay, if this is from midnight to midnight, does that make it an all-day event?' And not having to worry about those edge cases on the client. So, I'll probably have 'all day,' maybe it's a boolean. And in that case, start and end may or may not be a date time. We might not need the time. I think that's good as it is. Just kind of default it to midnight or some other time and then just use the date information if it's an all-day event. I think that would work well there, midnight in the user's time zone. Yeah, so depending on the database, kind of coming from my past experience, just thinking like if this was built out in Postgres, being able to use a timestamp with time zone to be able to have that extra information there, then we'd already have that and be able to work with that. Yeah, that makes sense. Sweet. Then just working down the list, the next thing would be notification. So, with a notification, what are the types of information that we need with that? ID, and then we would have the date time that that is going to, maybe we'll call that 'effective,' when the notification is supposed to go off. And that'll be scheduled beforehand, when the event is created or when the notification is set by the user. And then I'll worry about the relationships later. So, I think that's good there. Move this over.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Then the next thing, so I have a question about this: if the notification is associated with a specific event, do you have some way of indicating that here in this data model?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, definitely. I would likely have a join table or a referencing ID on one or either of the tables. I think after building out the notification channel table, I'll go through and map out those relationships next.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Sure, sounds good.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: So, notification channel here also ID. And then I would likely have, let's think, what would need to be on here? I'll move this around a little bit. So, we'd have probably the method, maybe this is a string and there's a list of options like email or SMS, etc. And then the value associated with that, so that would be the actual email address, that'd be the actual phone number, or maybe it's through some other channel and there's like an ID associated with how that is being sent off. And then I think that looks good there as far as the actual data. The next thing really is building out the relationships between these objects. I think I'll probably map these out as kind of like join tables or for the relationships between them, whether or not they're actually tables in the...

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: A database would depend on the specific use case and the pattern in the database, but you can look at that on a case-by-case basis. Starting off with user and calendar, a user could definitely have multiple calendars that they've created to separate out their different events: their work calendar, personal calendar, etc. So for the moment, I will map this as a one-to-many relationship, and I'll create a table here called `user_calendar`. On this table, there will be `user_id`, which will reference the `user_id`. Then there will be a calendar. So between calendar and user, the user has a reference to `user_calendar`, or we can look that up via the `user_id`. The same would work for the calendar. In this way, if we want to get all the calendars for a user, we have their `user_id`, we could do a lookup on this `user_calendar` table to get that information. Here, we're thinking of this as one user to many calendars.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: So, quick question about that. Is there a particular reason why you chose to add a join table `user_calendar` instead of having a foreign key in the calendar table that indicates which user that calendar belongs to?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: One thing that comes to mind with a join table is that you can have extra information associated with the relationship. So maybe there are different permissions associated with a user for each calendar. For example, if this was there, so maybe this user to this calendar, I guess in this case it's always the owner, but if we have shared calendars, maybe there's a permissions object here as well. So we could say, 'When I'm creating this, I know that the user is an editor or just a viewer on this calendar.' It's a place to add some extra information there.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, great. So that means that this design allows for a lot of extra flexibility going forward.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: It uses a little bit more space, but has some of that flexibility and slightly changes the way that you're going to access the data.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that makes a lot of sense.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Then moving on, we'll probably do something similar between the calendar and the events. So I have an `event_calendar`, and this will be very similar. We'll have `id`, which will reference the `calendar.id`, and then `event_id`, referencing the `event.id`. In this case, a calendar can definitely have many events, so that will be one-to-many. An event, because it can be shared, will also be one-to-many to this table. Thinking through the feature where you're sharing an event with someone, likely there will be an owner on the event, and you'll have this `calendar_event` here. But then likely, we'll need some other way to identify who the invitees are as well. So this is a situation where maybe we do want to include some extra information on the `calendar_event`. For example, we could have `role`, which would be a string. Then whenever an event is added to a calendar, we can know, 'Was this the person who created or organized the event, or are they an invitee?' So I think that looks good for the many-to-many relationship between calendars and events. The next thing to look at would be between events and notifications. I'll map this out as a join table, but I don't think I would necessarily make this an actual table in the database. I would probably just have `event_id` on the notification, just for consistency. The diagram will probably put it out like this. In this case, each event could have several notifications for it. Maybe you want one 30 minutes before, 10 minutes before, etc. Because an event can have multiple calendars and, by connection, users associated with it, it might also need the `user_id` associated with that notification. You could store that either in the `event_notification` table or the `notification` table. I'll probably put it here in the `notification` table. When the notification is created, we have the `user_id` associated with that. In that way, we can also get the notification channel for the user. So this will sit between, and this will need to have `user_id` in that case. Then we're able to look up, 'Okay, I have a notification for this time, what are the notification channels for that user?'

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: I'm just checking, so this is the user who receives some notification?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yes, that's correct. In that way, we can look up, 'What's the email that we should be sending this to? What is the SMS number?' etc. So in that case, we're able to go through. Looking back at our requirements, a user can create calendars, create events, and set reminders on those events, which will be stored as notifications. The time zone will be through the calendar, and then using that setting or metadata of the calendar, we'll be able to accurately set the event start and end date for them. An interesting thing is that if we're sharing these events and the date/time, maybe we have these as UTC actually. Then, because we have the time zone on the calendar, we're able to say per user, we can know a time zone there and then display that correctly. So this would actually probably be timestamped without time zone more accurately. Inviting users to events is covered. We've mapped out all of our entities. I think this covers the use cases and gives us an idea of the relationships I'd like to have between them.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, great. Thanks for going through all of that. I had a couple of follow-up questions about this. How would your design change if you wanted to add the ability to have a team calendar?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: That's a great question. We touched on this earlier. You would need some extra information between the user and the calendar. So likely that would mean creating a team table, so we have an `id`, and then we'd have a relationship between users and teams that would probably also be a many-to-many relationship. This would be `team_user` that would link those tables together. Then we'd have that same way going back. Instead of `user_calendar`, I think this might more generically be something like `organization_calendar` or `entity_calendar` because the calendar could be owned by a user or by a team. Depending on the type of the organization, you're going to be sending that information or sharing it with multiple other people. In addition to that, I would probably also have to consider adding roles and permissions as well. So I would likely map that out as separate tables where, starting from the base up, a permission would be the base action that someone is able to perform, and that would be something like a permission for creating an event, there would...

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: A permission for adding a team member, etc. Those permissions would then be associated with a role. So, a role would be like editor, admin, or viewer, etc. A role could have multiple permissions associated with it, and then each team user would likely have a role describing what actions they are able to take. So, those are a couple of things that would need to be added to be able to have team calendars.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, right. So, it looks like each role is associated with an individual team user.

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, so this would likely be a many-to-many relationship, maybe one-to-many. You know, maybe the team user can only have one role. So, likely, if we were to map this out, the team user would have a reference to the team ID, and then it would also have a reference to the user ID and then the role ID.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, yeah, that makes sense. Thanks for answering, but one last follow-up question is: How do you think this design would scale?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, one thing to always consider with a relational model like this is how you're going to be accessing the data. What big joins are going to happen? Which of these tables are going to grow the largest? Events is probably going to be a lot larger than calendar, for example, because the calendar is going to have a ton of different events. So, considering those, and then looking for any data that's hard to access that's going to require a bunch of joins. So, getting maybe a notification channel may be difficult because you're kind of going through, 'Okay, I have a calendar, I'm going to get all the events, I need to join that with notification, and then with user to get the notification channel.' So, effectively, thinking through what data do I have. In that case, getting the notification channel, we already kind of are denormalizing the data, which is a really handy technique to trade off speed versus space. In this case, a notification has a user ID, so that's kind of like extra information that would actually make getting the notification channel a lot faster.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, yeah, that makes a lot of sense. All right, so thank you so much for doing this mock interview with me. I think now is a good time for us to debrief. What did you think of this interview? How do you think you did? What do you think went well, and what do you think you would want to improve?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, definitely. I think I was able to think through the entities relatively well. I think clearly mapping out the relationships is always a challenge. It's something that I want to continue to get better at, having that foresight of knowing, based on how the application is going to be built out, using a join table versus foreign keys, and mapping that out so that it's clear how you're able to access the data and how things relate to each other. I think overall it went well, but definitely a lot of room for improvement.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Okay, well, first of all, congratulations. Personally, on my side, I thought that you did a really great job of clearly outlining what entities are involved in this problem. And then, in order to come up with the relationships, you did a really excellent job at describing how you can use the access patterns to determine what kind of relationships you need in between the entities. You also had great answers to my follow-up questions. One extra thing that I think would have made your answer even better is maybe if you had talked about which columns might be helpful to index. For example, we might want to query often for all of the events that belong to a particular calendar, which means that both event ID and calendar ID might be really great columns to index. That would make our queries a lot more efficient. So, what do you think?

turns.speaker: EMPLOYEE
turns.text: Yeah, that's awesome feedback. There are definitely a lot of other features and aspects of a relational model that I could have reached for when describing that, like indexes, or considering, or maybe it may or may not be relevant to this problem, but things like rollup tables, for example, could be another tool to consider when going through this sort of problem in the future.

turns.speaker: INTERVIEWER
turns.text: Yeah, that's a really good point. Thanks so much for joining us for this mock interview today, Connor. And for our viewers who are watching, good luck with your upcoming interviews. Bye everyone.

training_pairs.question: Connor, will you go ahead and introduce yourself?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, hi everyone. My name is Connor Lindsay. I'm a software engineer at Grafana Labs, currently working on our open-source observability software.

training_pairs.question: Our question today is: design a relational model for a calendar application. To get started, I just want to ask a couple of clarifying questions. I'm talking about a calendar application, I'd love to clarify what features specifically that we're looking to include. I'm assuming things like events, maybe notifications or reminders. Could you provide some more information there?
training_pairs.answer: Sure. The user should be able to add events, reminders, the time zone, and be able to eventually invite other users to the events.

training_pairs.question: If the notification is associated with a specific event, do you have some way of indicating that here in this data model?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, definitely. I would likely have a join table or a referencing ID on one or either of the tables. I think after building out the notification channel table, I'll go through and map out those relationships next.

training_pairs.question: Is there a particular reason why you chose to add a join table `user_calendar` instead of having a foreign key in the calendar table that indicates which user that calendar belongs to?
training_pairs.answer: One thing that comes to mind with a join table is that you can have extra information associated with the relationship. So maybe there are different permissions associated with a user for each calendar. For example, if this was there, so maybe this user to this calendar, I guess in this case it's always the owner, but if we have shared calendars, maybe there's a permissions object here as well. So we could say, 'When I'm creating this, I know that the user is an editor or just a viewer on this calendar.' It's a place to add some extra information there.

training_pairs.question: I'm just checking, so this is the user who receives some notification?
training_pairs.answer: Yes, that's correct. In that way, we can look up, 'What's the email that we should be sending this to? What is the SMS number?' etc. So in that case, we're able to go through. Looking back at our requirements, a user can create calendars, create events, and set reminders on those events, which will be stored as notifications. The time zone will be through the calendar, and then using that setting or metadata of the calendar, we'll be able to accurately set the event start and end date for them. An interesting thing is that if we're sharing these events and the date/time, maybe we have these as UTC actually. Then, because we have the time zone on the calendar, we're able to say per user, we can know a time zone there and then display that correctly. So this would actually probably be timestamped without time zone more accurately. Inviting users to events is covered. We've mapped out all of our entities. I think this covers the use cases and gives us an idea of the relationships I'd like to have between them.

training_pairs.question: How would your design change if you wanted to add the ability to have a team calendar?
training_pairs.answer: That's a great question. We touched on this earlier. You would need some extra information between the user and the calendar. So likely that would mean creating a team table, so we have an `id`, and then we'd have a relationship between users and teams that would probably also be a many-to-many relationship. This would be `team_user` that would link those tables together. Then we'd have that same way going back. Instead of `user_calendar`, I think this might more generically be something like `organization_calendar` or `entity_calendar` because the calendar could be owned by a user or by a team. Depending on the type of the organization, you're going to be sending that information or sharing it with multiple other people. In addition to that, I would probably also have to consider adding roles and permissions as well. So I would likely map that out as separate tables where, starting from the base up, a permission would be the base action that someone is able to perform, and that would be something like a permission for creating an event, there would...

training_pairs.question: Okay, right. So, it looks like each role is associated with an individual team user.
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, so this would likely be a many-to-many relationship, maybe one-to-many. You know, maybe the team user can only have one role. So, likely, if we were to map this out, the team user would have a reference to the team ID, and then it would also have a reference to the user ID and then the role ID.
training_pairs.follow_up: How do you think this design would scale?

training_pairs.question: How do you think this design would scale?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, one thing to always consider with a relational model like this is how you're going to be accessing the data. What big joins are going to happen? Which of these tables are going to grow the largest? Events is probably going to be a lot larger than calendar, for example, because the calendar is going to have a ton of different events. So, considering those, and then looking for any data that's hard to access that's going to require a bunch of joins. So, getting maybe a notification channel may be difficult because you're kind of going through, 'Okay, I have a calendar, I'm going to get all the events, I need to join that with notification, and then with user to get the notification channel.' So, effectively, thinking through what data do I have. In that case, getting the notification channel, we already kind of are denormalizing the data, which is a really handy technique to trade off speed versus space. In this case, a notification has a user ID, so that's kind of like extra information that would actually make getting the notification channel a lot faster.

training_pairs.question: What did you think of this interview? How do you think you did? What do you think went well, and what do you think you would want to improve?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, definitely. I think I was able to think through the entities relatively well. I think clearly mapping out the relationships is always a challenge. It's something that I want to continue to get better at, having that foresight of knowing, based on how the application is going to be built out, using a join table versus foreign keys, and mapping that out so that it's clear how you're able to access the data and how things relate to each other. I think overall it went well, but definitely a lot of room for improvement.

training_pairs.question: Okay, well, first of all, congratulations. Personally, on my side, I thought that you did a really great job of clearly outlining what entities are involved in this problem. And then, in order to come up with the relationships, you did a really excellent job at describing how you can use the access patterns to determine what kind of relationships you need in between the entities. You also had great answers to my follow-up questions. One extra thing that I think would have made your answer even better is maybe if you had talked about which columns might be helpful to index. For example, we might want to query often for all of the events that belong to a particular calendar, which means that both event ID and calendar ID might be really great columns to index. That would make our queries a lot more efficient. So, what do you think?
training_pairs.answer: Yeah, that's awesome feedback. There are definitely a lot of other features and aspects of a relational model that I could have reached for when describing that, like indexes, or considering, or maybe it may or may not be relevant to this problem, but things like rollup tables, for example, could be another tool to consider when going through this sort of problem in the future.
